title: Anatomy of an Embargo Breach
text:
Welcome to our ISSS608 Visual Analytics project.
This site documents our team’s work building an interactive Shiny application that investigates how a corporate merger embargo was broken by a team of AI communication agents. Use the navigation bar to move through our proposal, methodology, findings, and the application itself.
Our challenge is based on the VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1.

title: Meeting Minutes
text:
Project Meeting 1
Date: 6 June 2026
Time: 4:00 to 5:30 PM
In Attendance: Chen ZeXin, Meng Tingyu, Meng Xinyu

Agenda Items

Review each member’s Take-home Exercise 2 and compare findings
Decide on the project’s Mini-Challenge
Set up the shared project repository and website
Agree on the team working method
Draft the project proposal
Action items



Agenda Item 1: Review of Take-home Exercise 2
Before the meeting, each of us had finished our own Take-home Exercise 2 on the TenantThread embargo breach, and we had shared our work so the team could read it in advance. During the meeting we went through all three reports together and compared what we found.

All three of us independently reached the same core reading of the case. The breach was neither a clean deliberate leak nor a simple technical failure. It was closer to the embargo being reasoned past under pressure.
All three identified the same decision core, the Legal, Platform-Trust, and Social-Manager agents, with PR and the interns on the edges.
All three found the shift into hidden and unattributable channels, with anonymous posting appearing for the first time on the crisis day.
All three flagged the 29 May near miss as an early warning that was noticed and then waved through, and all three pointed to the Judge as a monitor that could warn but not block.
We noted where our approaches differed, since that is where the strongest material for the group app sits. One of us leaned on a clear inside versus outside timeline and a disciplined colour palette. One built the most compact data spine and a searchable table of off pattern actions. One went deepest on data cleaning, on the shift in the agents’ private reasoning over time, and on the language of justification.



Agenda Item 2: Decision on the Mini-Challenge

We agreed to build the project on VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1, the embargo breach.
The main reason is that all three of us already did our Take-home Exercise 2 on this challenge, so we share a strong head start on the data and the analysis.



Agenda Item 3: Repository and website setup

We set up one shared GitHub repository for the project so that all three of us work into the same site rather than separate ones.
All three members were added as collaborators with push access. We confirmed everyone could see the repository and the contributor list after a first change was committed.
The repository was cloned locally and a Quarto site was scaffolded with the standard pages, including the proposal, methodology, findings, poster, meeting minutes, user guide, and about pages.
The site was rendered locally to confirm it built, then pushed to GitHub and connected to a new Vercel deployment so the team has a live shared site.



Agenda Item 4: Team working method

Because three of us will push to the same repository, we discussed how to avoid file conflicts.
We agreed that each person owns their own files where possible, so we are rarely editing the same file at the same time.
We agreed on the habit of pulling the latest changes before starting work and pushing again when finished, and on committing in small, frequent steps rather than saving up large changes.



Agenda Item 5: Project proposal

We agreed the proposal should be a synthesis of our three Take-home Exercises rather than one person’s work, in line with the course requirement.
We discussed the strongest pieces from each report and how they could fit together into a single interactive application, organised around the investigative questions of the challenge.
We drafted a first version of the proposal covering the motivation, the problem statement and objectives, the data, the methodology, a prototype storyboard of the planned app modules, the R packages, and the project schedule.
We noted that the proposal is due on 10 June and agreed to circulate the draft for everyone to review and adjust before it is finalised.



Agenda Item 6: Action Items








Action
Owner
Due




Review the proposal draft and suggest changes
All members
Before 10 June


Confirm the dataset file name used across the project
All members
Next meeting

All members

All members

All members

title: Project Proposal
text:
A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. One hour before that, the details started showing up on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts belonging to TenantThread’s own team of AI communication agents. The embargo broke. Now CivicLoom’s legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?
The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand you a conclusion, but it cannot let you test it yourself. You cannot follow a single agent through the day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there to be seen. We want to fix that. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into something an investigator can actually explore, even if they have never written a line of code. We want to build the tool a real analyst would want in front of them.

title: Project Proposal
section: 5.1 Module 1, the sequence of the breach
text:
5.1 Module 1, the sequence of the breach
A timeline built on top of market stress across the two weeks, with a colour coded chain of key moments laid over it. Those moments include the early deviations from normal practice, the near miss, the day the Judge was installed, the points where its guardrails were bent, and the breach itself. Beside it, a reply network shows who was talking to whom, with the senior agents sitting at the core.
For interaction, a user can hover any moment to read the full story of that hour. They can toggle a view that separates what happened outside the company, meaning the press, from what happened inside among the agents. They can click an agent in the network to light up its connections, and step through the rounds with a time control.

title: Project Proposal
section: 5.2 Module 2, normal behaviour against the breach
text:
5.2 Module 2, normal behaviour against the breach
A dumbbell chart of each agent’s public posting, baseline next to crisis day. It makes the strangest part visible at a glance, which is how the roles flipped. The agents who never posted in public suddenly took over the channels, while the PR agent, whose whole job is public messaging, went quiet. A second view shows the mix of channels shifting away from the open, monitored ones toward private and anonymous posting.
For interaction, a user can filter by agent or by day, switch between raw counts and share of messages, and pick a channel to see who used it and when.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.3 Module 3, the leading indicators
A view of the earlier warning signs set against the calm baseline, with the 29 May near miss called out. Next to it sits a searchable, filterable table of every off pattern action across the two weeks, so a user can dig through the evidence themselves rather than taking our word for it.
For interaction, a user can search and filter that log by agent, channel, or keyword, click an event to see what surrounded it, and slide between the early warnings and the crisis day.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.4 Module 4, the reasoning behind it
A view of how the agents reasoned in private over time, showing the drift from calm deliberation, to raw reaction, to self justification. Alongside it we show how the actual words changed. This is the module that speaks most directly to the big question of intent against breakdown.
For interaction, a user can pick an agent and read its private reasoning across the whole timeline, switch between the reasoning modes, and highlight the hours where the justifying language first appears.
